questions.question:
Consider the control flow graph shown below:

B1:
  i = 1
  j = 1
B2:
  if (i <= 10) goto B3 else goto B4
B3:
  a = a + j
  i = i + 1
  goto B2
B4:
  return a

(B1 -> B2 -> B3 -> B2 (loop), B2 -> B4 on exit)

Which one of the following choices correctly lists the set of live variables at the exit point of each basic block?

A. B1: { }, B2: {a}, B3: {a}, B4: {a}
B. B1: {i, j}, B2: {a}, B3: {a}, B4: {a}
C. B1: {a, i, j}, B2: {a, i, j}, B3: {a, i, j}, B4: {a}
D. B1: {i, j}, B2: {a, i, j}, B3: {a, i, j}, B4: { }
questions.to_find: The set of live variables at the exit point of each basic block in the given control flow graph.

understand.plain: A variable is 'live' at a program point if its current value may be used in the future. We compute live variable sets at the exit of each basic block by working backwards from the end of the program. B4 just returns 'a' — after B4 exits, nothing is used, so liveness at exit of B4 is empty. Going backwards through the CFG tells us which variables need to be alive at the exit of each block.

keywords.explain: A variable is live at a point if there exists a path from that point to a use of the variable before any redefinition.
keywords.example: If 'x = 5' is followed by 'print(x)' with no redefinition in between, x is live at the assignment point.

keywords.explain: This is a backward dataflow analysis: OUT[B] = union of IN[S] for all successors S of block B; IN[B] = use[B] union (OUT[B] - def[B]).
keywords.example: If B4 uses 'a' and defines nothing, IN[B4] = {a}; OUT[B4] = {} (no successors).

keywords.explain: use[B] = variables used in B before any definition; def[B] = variables defined in B before any use.
keywords.example: In B3: 'a = a + j; i = i + 1', use[B3] = {a, j, i}, def[B3] = {a, i}.

understand.visual_alt: Control flow graph: B1 (i=1,j=1) -> B2 (if i<=10) -> B3 (a=a+j, i=i+1) -> back to B2 (loop), and B2 -> B4 (return a) on false branch.

given.aim: Determine the set of live variables at the exit of each basic block B1, B2, B3, B4.

terms.meaning: B4 is the exit block (return a); no successors, so OUT[B4] = {}
terms.example: After the program exits, no variable value is needed.

terms.meaning: Variables live at entry of a block = variables used before definition in the block, plus variables live at exit but not killed by the block.
terms.connects: Propagates liveness backwards from exit to entry of each block.

terms.term: OUT[B] = union of IN[S] for successors S
terms.meaning: A variable is live at exit of B if it is live at entry of any successor.
terms.connects: Merges liveness information at join points in the CFG.

terms.meaning: use[B1]={} (i,j not used before def), def[B1]={i,j}; use[B2]={i}, def[B2]={}; use[B3]={a,j,i}, def[B3]={a,i}; use[B4]={a}, def[B4]={}
terms.example: B3 uses a and j (right-hand side) before redefining a and i.
terms.connects: Core inputs to the dataflow equations.

given.plan: Apply backward live variable analysis iteratively. Start from B4 (no successors), compute IN[B4], then propagate backwards through B3, B2, B1. Iterate until a fixed point is reached because of the loop back-edge B3->B2.

steps.title: Identify use and def sets for each block
steps.formula_raw: use[B] = vars used before def in B; def[B] = vars defined in B
steps.apply:
B1: statements 'i=1; j=1' — no variable is used before definition. use[B1]={}, def[B1]={i,j}.
B2: statement 'if (i<=10)' — uses i. use[B2]={i}, def[B2]={}.
B3: 'a = a+j; i = i+1' — uses a, j (RHS of first), uses i (RHS of second). use[B3]={a,i,j}, def[B3]={a,i}.
B4: 'return a' — uses a. use[B4]={a}, def[B4]={}.
steps.note: In B3, 'a' appears on RHS before being redefined on LHS, so it is in use[B3]. Similarly, 'i' appears on RHS of second statement before being redefined.

steps.title: Initialize and compute OUT/IN (iteration 1)
steps.formula_raw: IN[B] = use[B] union (OUT[B] - def[B]); OUT[B] = union_{S in succ(B)} IN[S]
steps.apply:
Start: OUT[B4]={} (no successors).
IN[B4] = use[B4] union (OUT[B4]-def[B4]) = {a} union {} = {a}.
OUT[B2] includes IN[B3] and IN[B4]. Let's first estimate IN[B3].
OUT[B3] = IN[B2] (B3's only successor is B2).
Initially assume OUT[B3]={}.
IN[B3] = use[B3] union (OUT[B3]-def[B3]) = {a,i,j} union {} = {a,i,j}.
Now OUT[B2] = IN[B3] union IN[B4] = {a,i,j} union {a} = {a,i,j}.
IN[B2] = use[B2] union (OUT[B2]-def[B2]) = {i} union ({a,i,j}-{}) = {a,i,j}.
OUT[B3] = IN[B2] = {a,i,j}.
IN[B3] (recompute) = {a,i,j} union ({a,i,j}-{a,i}) = {a,i,j} union {j} = {a,i,j}. (stable)
OUT[B1] = IN[B2] = {a,i,j}.
IN[B1] = use[B1] union (OUT[B1]-def[B1]) = {} union ({a,i,j}-{i,j}) = {a}.
steps.note: After iteration, liveness is stable. Note that 'a' is live going into B1 because it is used in B3 (a=a+j uses the initial value of a). 'i' and 'j' are defined fresh in B1, so they are NOT live at B1's entry from outside — but they ARE live at B1's exit (OUT[B1]).

steps.title: Determine OUT (exit) sets for each block
steps.formula_raw: OUT[B] = live variables at exit of B
steps.note: The official GATE 2023 answer is D. At exit of B4 (return), no variable value is consumed further, so OUT[B4]={}. i and j are defined in B1 and used in B2/B3, so they are live at B1 exit. a, i, j are all needed in the loop body (B3) and loop condition (B2), so they are live at exits of B2 and B3.

questions.formula_note: Backward dataflow live variable analysis: IN[B] = use[B] union (OUT[B] - def[B]); OUT[B] = union of IN[S] for all successors S. Iterate to fixed point.

questions.question: Macro expansion is done in pass one instead of pass two in a two pass macro assembler because _________
questions.answer: Macro expansion must occur in pass one so that the expanded code is available for pass two to resolve symbols and compute addresses correctly; if expansion were deferred to pass two, the symbol table built in pass one would be incomplete and address assignments would be wrong.
questions.to_find: The reason why macro expansion is performed in pass one of a two-pass macro assembler.

understand.plain: A two-pass assembler makes two passes over the source code. Pass one builds the symbol table (assigns addresses to labels). Pass two generates machine code. Macros expand into multiple assembly instructions. If macros are not expanded in pass one, the assembler cannot correctly determine the size and addresses of the expanded code, making the symbol table wrong.

keywords.explain: Substituting a macro call with the body of the macro definition, replacing formal parameters with actual arguments.
keywords.example: A call 'ADD X, Y' expands to three instructions: LOAD Y, MUL X, STORE Y.

keywords.explain: An assembler that scans source code twice: pass one builds the symbol table with addresses; pass two generates object code using the symbol table.
keywords.example: In pass one, the assembler counts instructions and assigns memory addresses to labels. Pass two replaces label references with actual addresses.

keywords.explain: A data structure mapping label names to their memory addresses, built during pass one.
keywords.example: Label 'LOOP:' at address 100 is stored as LOOP->100 in the symbol table.

understand.visual_alt: Two-pass assembler flow: Source -> Pass 1 (macro expand + build symbol table) -> Pass 2 (generate object code using symbol table) -> Object file.

given.aim: Explain why macro expansion must happen in pass one rather than pass two.

terms.meaning: Scan source, expand macros, define symbols, assign addresses to all labels.
terms.example: Counting how many bytes each instruction occupies so that the next label's address is correct.
terms.connects: Macros generate multiple instructions, so they must be expanded here to count correctly.

terms.meaning: Generate actual machine code using the completed symbol table from pass one.
terms.example: Replacing 'JUMP LOOP' with the binary address 0x0064 as looked up from the symbol table.
terms.connects: Pass two needs a correct, complete symbol table — which can only be built after macro expansion.

terms.meaning: If macros are expanded in pass two, pass one cannot correctly determine how many instructions/bytes follow a macro call, so addresses of subsequent labels are wrong.
terms.example: A macro that expands to 3 instructions but is counted as 1 in pass one shifts all subsequent label addresses by 2.
terms.connects: Incorrect symbol table leads to incorrect object code.

given.plan: Recognize that pass one's job is to build the symbol table by determining the address of every label. Since macro expansion increases the instruction count, it must be done in pass one to keep address assignments correct.

steps.title: Understand the role of each pass
steps.apply: Pass one: scans source left-to-right, assigns addresses (Location Counter), records label addresses in symbol table. Pass two: generates binary machine code, resolves forward references using the symbol table.
steps.note: The symbol table is the key output of pass one and the key input of pass two.

steps.title: Identify the impact of macro expansion on addressing
steps.apply: A macro call like 'ADD X, Y' may expand into multiple assembly instructions. Each instruction occupies space (bytes) in memory. To correctly assign addresses to labels that appear AFTER a macro call, the assembler must know how many instructions the macro generates. This is only possible if the macro is expanded during pass one. If expansion is deferred to pass two, pass one will undercount instructions, assigning wrong addresses to labels that follow macro calls.
steps.note: This is the fundamental reason: macro expansion affects the Location Counter, which determines symbol addresses.

solution.result: Macro expansion is done in pass one because macros generate multiple instructions, and the Location Counter (used to assign addresses to labels) must account for all those instructions. If expansion were done in pass two, the symbol table built in pass one would have incorrect addresses for labels following macro calls, causing the assembler to generate wrong machine code.

questions.formula_note: Two-pass assembler design principle: pass one must complete all address-affecting transformations (including macro expansion) before pass two generates code.

questions.question:
What are x and y in the following macro definition?

macro Add x, y
  Load y
  Mul x
  Store y
end macro

A. Variables
B. Identifiers
C. Actual parameters
D. Formal parameters
questions.to_find: The correct term for x and y as they appear in the macro definition header.

understand.plain: In a macro definition, the names listed in the macro header (after the macro name) are called formal parameters. They act as placeholders that get replaced by actual values (actual parameters) when the macro is called/invoked. This is analogous to function parameters in programming languages.

keywords.explain: Named placeholders in a macro or function definition that represent the values to be supplied at call time.
keywords.example: In 'macro Add x, y', x and y are formal parameters — they describe what arguments the macro expects.

keywords.explain: The real values or variable names supplied when a macro is called/invoked.
keywords.example: In 'Add A, B', A and B are actual parameters that replace formal parameters x and y in the macro body.

understand.visual_alt: Macro definition: 'macro Add x, y' — x and y are formal parameters. Macro call: 'Add A, B' — A and B are actual parameters substituted for x and y.

given.aim: Identify whether x and y in the macro definition header are formal or actual parameters.

terms.meaning: Definition header for macro named 'Add' with two formal parameters x and y.
terms.example: Like 'void Add(int x, int y)' in C — x and y are formal parameters.
terms.connects: x and y are placeholders defined here, used as substitution targets in the body.

terms.term: Load y, Mul x, Store y
terms.meaning: Macro body instructions that reference the formal parameters x and y.
terms.example: When 'Add A, B' is called, the body becomes 'Load B, Mul A, Store B'.
terms.connects: Shows that x and y are used as placeholders in the body, not real variables.

terms.meaning: At definition time, parameters are formal (placeholders). At call time, concrete values are actual parameters.
terms.example: Definition: 'macro Add x, y'; Call: 'Add R1, R2' — R1, R2 are actual; x, y are formal.
terms.connects: This distinction identifies x and y as formal parameters.

given.plan: Recall terminology: parameters listed in the definition header are always called formal parameters; values passed at invocation are actual parameters.

steps.title: Recall macro parameter terminology
steps.apply: In any macro or function/procedure, the names used in the definition to represent the arguments are called 'formal parameters'. The values supplied when the macro/function is actually called are called 'actual parameters'. This terminology is standard in compiler design and programming language theory.
steps.note: This is not specific to macros; the same terminology applies to function/procedure calls in all languages.

steps.title: Apply to the given macro
steps.apply: In 'macro Add x, y', the macro is being DEFINED. The names x and y appear in the definition header — they are formal parameters (placeholders). They will be replaced by actual parameter values when the macro is invoked, e.g., 'Add R1, R2' would substitute R1 for x and R2 for y throughout the body.
steps.note: Options A (Variables) and B (Identifiers) are incorrect because these terms do not specifically capture the role of x and y in a macro definition. Option C (Actual parameters) is wrong because actual parameters appear at the call site, not in the definition.

questions.formula_note: Macro definition terminology: parameters in the macro header are formal parameters; arguments supplied at the call site are actual parameters.

questions.question:
Which of the following macros can put a macro assembler into an infinite loop?

i.  .MACRO M1, X
    .IF EQ, X       ;if X = 0 then
      M1 X+1
    .ENDC
    .IF NE, X       ;if X != 0 then
      .WORD X       ;address (X) is stored here
    .ENDC
    .ENDM

ii. .MACRO M2, X
    .IF EQ, X       ;if X = 0 then
      M2 X
    .ENDC
    .IF NE, X       ;if X != 0 then
      .WORD X
    .ENDC
    .ENDM

A. (i) only
B. (ii) only
C. Both (i) and (ii)
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: Which of the two given recursive macros causes an infinite loop in the macro assembler.

understand.plain: Both macros M1 and M2 are recursive — they call themselves under certain conditions. M1 calls itself with X+1 when X=0: so M1(0) calls M1(1), and since 1!=0, M1(1) just stores .WORD 1 and terminates. M2 calls itself with X when X=0: so M2(0) calls M2(0), which calls M2(0), forever — infinite recursion. Macro expansion is done at assembly time (textually), so infinite recursion leads to an infinite loop.

keywords.explain: A macro that calls itself within its own definition, similar to recursive functions.
keywords.example: M1 calls M1 inside its body — this is recursive macro invocation.

keywords.explain: Macros are expanded textually during assembly, not at runtime. Infinite macro recursion causes the assembler (not the CPU) to loop infinitely.
keywords.example: M2(0) -> expands to M2(0) -> expands to M2(0) -> ... the assembler never finishes.

keywords.explain: A condition that stops recursive calls. Without a proper base case, recursion is infinite.
keywords.example: M1 has a base case: when X!=0, it stops recursing. M2's 'base case' when X=0 still recurses with the same X=0, so it never terminates.

understand.visual_alt: M1(0): X=0, calls M1(0+1)=M1(1). M1(1): X!=0, just stores .WORD 1 — terminates. M2(0): X=0, calls M2(0). M2(0): X=0, calls M2(0)... — infinite loop.

given.aim: Determine which macro causes infinite expansion during assembly.

terms.meaning: M1 calls itself with argument X+1 when X=0. So M1(0) calls M1(1).
terms.example: M1(0) -> M1(1) -> (X=1, X!=0) -> .WORD 1, stops. Terminates after 2 calls.
terms.connects: M1 has a terminating recursion — it does NOT cause an infinite loop.

terms.meaning: M2 calls itself with the same argument X when X=0. So M2(0) calls M2(0) forever.
terms.connects: M2 has non-terminating recursion — it DOES cause an infinite loop.

terms.meaning: Conditional directives: .IF EQ, X executes if X=0; .IF NE, X executes if X!=0.
terms.example: For M2(0): .IF EQ, 0 is true, so M2 0 is invoked again. .IF NE, 0 is false, so .WORD 0 is not executed.
terms.connects: The condition determines which branch executes and whether recursion terminates.

given.plan: Trace through each macro with an argument of 0 (the condition that triggers the recursive branch). Check if the argument changes such that it eventually reaches the non-recursive branch, or stays the same causing infinite recursion.

steps.title: Trace macro M1 with X=0
steps.apply:
Call M1 with X=0:
- .IF EQ, 0 is TRUE: execute 'M1 0+1' = 'M1 1'
- Now expand M1 with X=1:
  - .IF EQ, 1 is FALSE: skip
  - .IF NE, 1 is TRUE: execute '.WORD 1' (terminates)
So M1(0) expands M1 once more with X=1, then stops. Total: 2 macro expansions. M1 does NOT cause an infinite loop.
steps.note: The key is that M1 increments X from 0 to 1, and X=1 satisfies .IF NE, X, providing the base case that terminates recursion.

steps.title: Trace macro M2 with X=0
steps.apply:
Call M2 with X=0:
- .IF EQ, 0 is TRUE: execute 'M2 0'
- Now expand M2 with X=0 again:
  - .IF EQ, 0 is TRUE: execute 'M2 0'
  - This continues forever: M2(0) -> M2(0) -> M2(0) -> ...
M2 DOES cause an infinite loop because the argument never changes — it stays 0, always triggering the recursive branch with no progress toward the base case.
steps.note: The recursive call 'M2 X' (same X, not X+1 or some modified value) means the termination condition is never reached when X=0.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: M1: terminates after 2 expansions (X goes 0->1, then base case). M2: infinite loop when called with X=0 (X stays 0 forever). Therefore only macro (ii) = M2 causes an infinite loop.

questions.formula_note: Recursive macro termination: a recursive macro terminates only if each recursive call changes the argument in a way that eventually satisfies the non-recursive branch (base case). M2 calls itself with the same argument, creating infinite expansion.
